body.title: [triage] Hex's inference slot is a CONSTRAINED RESOURCE with no owner, no capacity accounting, and no way to refuse — every consumer (tests, DS, aider, Granny's builders, the intent extractor) can send it unbounded work
body.intention: I intend that a constrained shared resource has exactly one owner that knows its capacity and can REFUSE work, so that no consumer — test, device, or human — can drive it past what it can serve. [intention_date: 2026-07-13]
body.description:
**RAISED BY AKIEN 2026-07-13:** "when we're dealing with constrained resources, we're
supposed to monitor them and consume them with care."

**AGREED — AND THE STATED FIX (run tests in series) IS TOO NARROW. This is the important part.**

Akien's framing was that we should not run tests "in massive parallel batches" over constrained resources.
**But the nine pytest runs that saturated Hex were NOT a parallel batch.** They were launched **SERIALLY**,
one at a time, across an afternoon. Each blocked and never exited. **A serialize-tests rule would have
changed nothing — every one of them WAS in series.** What accumulated was not concurrency; it was
**non-termination**. Recording this because the narrow rule would have given false comfort.

**THE TESTS WERE NEVER THE REAL PROBLEM — THEY JUST GOT THERE FIRST.** `llama-server` on Hex runs `-np 1`:
**one slot.** The consumers of that slot are DS, aider, the intent extractor, Granny's builders, and the test
suite. **None of them knows the capacity. None of them can be refused. None of them will be "run in series"
with each other by fiat.** The next pile-up will not be pytest.

**SO THE PRIMITIVE IS A LEASE, NOT A RULE.** A constrained resource needs an OWNER that knows its capacity
and can say no. Serial execution then falls out for free as "a lease with capacity 1" — but the lease is what
binds EVERY consumer, and a policy binds only the ones who read it.

The natural owner is the **inference device** — it already owns routing; it should own the slot budget too.
It is the one place every consumer already passes through.

**Scope boundary:** IN — (a) a capacity model for each inference source (Hex = 1 slot, cloud = N, etc.);
(b) an admission/lease primitive every consumer acquires before dispatch, which BLOCKS or REFUSES rather than
queueing without bound; (c) the health signal reports depth/occupancy, not merely reachability (pairs with
T-healthmonitor-probes-reachability-not-capability); (d) refusal is a NAMED, first-class outcome —
`refused: no capacity` must never collapse into `timed out`. OUT — the test-suite fix (done,
T-default-suite-drives-live-inference-and-saturates-hex); process reaping (T-orphan-watchdog-watches-tickets-not-processes).

**Test plan / discriminating fixture:** a source with capacity 1 and two concurrent consumers — the second is
REFUSED or BLOCKED, never admitted to a queue it cannot be served from. And the refusal is distinguishable in
the record from a timeout, because they have different fixes.

**Completion criteria:**
  1. A consumer cannot exceed a source's declared capacity — proven with a capacity-1 fixture and 2 consumers.
  2. `refused: no capacity` is a distinct recorded outcome from `timed out`.
  3. The capacity of each live source is stated as a NUMBER, discovered or configured, not assumed.
  4. Every existing consumer (DS, aider, intent extractor, tests) goes through the lease — grep-proven, no
     bypass path.
